title: AWS Releases Loom, an Open-Source Reference Platform for Governing AI Agents at Enterprise Scale
gist: AWS released Loom, an open-source, opinionated reference platform showing how enterprises can build and govern AI agents with security baked in—identity propagation via RFC 8693 token exchange, no runtime code generation, tag-based governance—positioning the platform-governance layer for custom agent workloads distinct from Anthropic's coding-tool gateway.

insights.claim: AWS positions Loom explicitly as a concrete open-source example of how organizations can build their own agent platforms, not as a managed service, built with Strands Agents SDK on Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Runtime.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: AWS is seeding demand for AgentCore consumption via a free reference platform; the revenue sits in the managed services underneath.
insights.quote: AWS positions it explicitly as a concrete example of how organizations might build their own agent platforms, not as a managed service.

insights.claim: Loom is free and open source with the cost residing in the managed services underneath it, and it graduated from a Heeki Park prototype documented on Medium in June before reaching AWS Labs.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: The give-away-the-platform, monetize-the-runtime model mirrors classic cloud loss-leader strategy.
insights.quote: Loom itself is free and open source; the cost sits in the managed services underneath it.
insights.locator: Loom itself is free and open source

themes.theme: Identity propagation as the hard problem

insights.claim: Loom's hardest solved problem is identity propagation: when an agent acts for a user and calls an MCP server that calls a REST API, each hop needs a token preserving the originating user's identity and permissions.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Delegated-identity propagation is the key unsolved enterprise blocker for agent adoption; whoever nails it wins governance mindshare.
insights.quote: Identity propagation is where Loom addresses the hardest problem on that list.
insights.locator: Identity propagation is where Loom addresses

insights.claim: Loom implements the full authorization code flow and uses RFC 8693 token exchange via AgentCore Identity so both end-user and agent identity travel in downstream tokens, exposing only data the originating user is permitted to access.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Standards-based (RFC 8693) delegation is the emerging blueprint for secure agent chains; a pattern to expect across vendors.
insights.locator: Loom implements the full authorization code flow

insights.claim: Loom takes a deliberate stance against runtime code generation, deploying a pre-written configurable Python agent where code never changes between deployments—only configuration does—so platform teams scan the code once and reuse it everywhere.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Config-over-codegen is a security-driven design choice countering the trend toward dynamic agent code; a differentiator for regulated buyers.
insights.quote: Code never changes between deployments; only configuration does.
insights.locator: The deployment model takes a deliberate stance

insights.claim: Secrets and credentials are never stored in Loom, living in AWS Secrets Manager and pulled only when required, with inbound and outbound auth managed by AgentCore Identity.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Externalized secrets is a baseline enterprise security requirement; reinforces AWS service pull-through.
insights.locator: Secrets and credentials are not stored in Loom at all.

insights.claim: Loom's blog structures the platform around seven agent-scaling challenges: resource tagging, RBAC/ABAC access, deployment blueprints, pre-deployment validation, identity propagation through delegated actor chains, agent sprawl, and human review before sensitive actions.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: A useful checklist of the real enterprise agent-governance problems, mapping the requirements any competing platform must meet.
insights.locator: The blog post structures Loom around seven challenges

insights.claim: Loom enforces governance via mandatory tag profiles (loom:application, loom:group, loom:owner) on every deployed resource plus two-dimensional access control combining role type and group tags.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Tag-enforced governance is the practical mechanism for controlling agent sprawl and cost attribution.

insights.claim: Human-in-the-loop review is implemented three ways using the Strands Agents hook framework and native MCP elicitations, pausing sensitive tool invocations for human approval before executing.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: MCP elicitations emerging as the standard mechanism for agent approval gates.
insights.locator: Human-in-the-loop review is implemented three ways

insights.claim: Loom lands in a quickly forming category alongside Anthropic's Claude apps gateway, which claims the access/cost-control layer for AI coding tools while Loom demonstrates the platform layer for custom agent workloads.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: The agent-governance market is bifurcating into coding-tool control (Anthropic) vs custom-workload platform (AWS); map vendors to these layers.
insights.quote: Loom lands in a quickly forming category.
insights.locator: Loom lands in a quickly forming category.

insights.claim: The maturity gap matters: Anthropic's gateway is a supported product while Loom is an AWS Labs reference implementation for greenfield builds whose role-based access model still carries visible demo scaffolding.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Loom is not production-ready out of the box; buyers face a build-vs-adopt decision, tempering near-term impact.
insights.quote: its role-based access model still carries visible demo scaffolding

insights.claim: Early community reaction to Loom was wait-and-see, with a Reddit thread drawing no deployment reports two days in and one commenter noting you could probably build your own version in a week.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: The 'build-it-yourself-in-a-week' skepticism is the adoption risk for reference implementations.
insights.quote: You could probably build your own version in a week though.
insights.locator: Early community reaction reflects a wait-and-see stance.
